Some Considerations in Reply Eulogistic of the Flesh. It Was Created by God. The Body of Man Was, in Fact, Previous to His Soul.

ccel.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

title: Some Considerations in Reply Eulogistic of the Flesh. It Was Created by God. The Body of Man Was, in Fact, Previous to His Soul.

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Inasmuch as all uneducated men, therefore, still form their opinions after these common-sense views, and as the falterers and the weak-minded have a renewal of their perplexities occasioned by the selfsame views; and as the first battering-ram which is directed against ourselves is that which shatters the condition of the flesh, we must on our side necessarily so manage our defences, as to guard, first of all, the condition of the flesh, their disparagement of it being repulsed by our own eulogy.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The heretics, therefore, challenged us to use our rhetoric no less than our philosophy.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Respecting, then, this frail and poor, worthless body, which they do not indeed hesitate to call evil, even if it had been the work of angels, as Menander and Marcus are pleased to think, or the formation of some fiery being, an angel, as Apelles teaches, it would be quite enough for securing respect for the body, that it had the support and protection of even a secondary deity.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The angels, we know, rank next to God.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now, whatever be the supreme God of each heretic, I should not unfairly derive the dignity of the flesh likewise from Him to whom was present the will for its production.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For, of course, if He had not willed its production, He would have prohibited it, when He knew it was in progress.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: It follows, then, that even on their principle the flesh is equally the work of God.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: There is no work but belongs to Him who has permitted it to exist.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: It is indeed a happy circumstance, that most of their doctrines, including even the harshest, accord to our God the entire formation of man.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: How mighty He is, you know full well who believe that He is the only God.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Let, then, the flesh begin to give you pleasure, since the Creator thereof is so great.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But, you say, even the world is the work of God, and yet “the fashion of this world passeth away,” as the apostle himself testifies; nor must it be predetermined that the world will be restored, simply because it is the work of God.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: And surely if the universe, after its ruin, is not to be formed again, why should a portion of it be?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: You are right, if a portion is on an equality with the whole.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But we maintain that there is a difference.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: In the first place, because all things were made by the Word of God, and without Him was nothing made.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now the flesh, too, had its existence from the Word of God, because of the principle, that here should be nothing without that Word.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: “Let us make man,” said He, before He created him, and added, “with our hand,” for the sake of his pre-eminence, that so he might not be compared with the rest of creation.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: And “God,” says (the Scripture), “formed man.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: There is undoubtedly a great difference in the procedure, springing of course from the nature of the case.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For the creatures which were made were inferior to him for whom they were made; and they were made for man, to whom they were afterwards made subject by God.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Rightly, therefore, had the creatures which were thus intended for subjection, come forth into being at the bidding and command and sole power of the divine voice; whilst man, on the contrary, destined to be their lord, was formed by God Himself, to the intent that he might be able to exercise his mastery, being created by the Master the Lord Himself.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Remember, too, that man is properly called flesh, which had a prior occupation in man’s designation: “And God formed man the clay of the ground.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: He now became man, who was hitherto clay.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: “And He breathed upon his face the breath of life, and man (that is, the clay) became a living soul; and God placed the man whom He had formed in the garden.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: So that man was clay at first, and only afterwards man entire.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: I wish to impress this on your attention, with a view to your knowing, that whatever God has at all purposed or promised to man, is due not to the soul simply, but to the flesh also; if not arising out of any community in their origin, yet at all events by the privilege possessed by the latter in its name.
